worth_it_explanation: Audience setup decides who an account reaches and which signals automated bidding learns from, so a weak setup limits performance whether or not anyone notices it in the reports. The checks here are quick. The audience tables in the campaign and ad group views show which segments are attached, whether each one is set to target or observe, and whether expansion or optimized targeting is widening reach past the segments the practitioner chose. Each finding points to a setting that can be changed in the audience manager or the campaign settings, not to a rebuild. The reach and the bidding signals improve from the day the setting is corrected.
intro: Audience setup in Google Ads controls who an account reaches and feeds the conversion signals that automated bidding learns from. The same controls can also work against an account when reach lists are missing, when an audience set to restrict reach over-narrows the campaign, or when expansion settings widen reach past the segments the practitioner intended. These checks confirm that remarketing and Customer Match lists exist and are used, that audience segments are attached in the mode the campaign needs, and that reach-widening features are deliberate rather than left on by default.

audit_checks.title: No remarketing or Customer Match lists are attached to active campaigns
audit_checks.impact_rationale: First-party reach lists are the segments an account already earned, and an account that runs without them gives up its warmest audiences and one of the stronger conversion signals it could feed automated bidding. The spend still runs, so the absence shows up as performance left on the table rather than as a visible error.

educate.base:
Remarketing lists are audiences built from people who already interacted with a site or app, for example past visitors or cart abandoners. Customer Match lists are audiences built from first-party contact data the advertiser uploads, such as a list of hashed customer emails. Both are first-party reach lists, and both can serve two roles: a campaign can target them directly to reach those people, or attach them in observation to read how they perform without narrowing reach.

An account that runs without these lists reaches only the audiences Google assembles, and it passes up the segments it already earned through past visits and existing customers. Those segments tend to convert at a higher rate than cold reach, so their absence shows up as weaker efficiency rather than as a broken setting. Automated bidding also loses a signal since first-party segments attached in observation tell the bidding which users resemble known converters.

Two setup gaps lead here. The account may have no audience lists built at all, which shows as an empty or near-empty audience manager. Or the lists exist but no active campaign attaches them, so they sit unused. A list that exists but is not attached does nothing for reach or bidding.

A few signs point to first-party lists being missing or unused:
  • The audience manager shows no remarketing or Customer Match segments, or only segments that are too small to serve
  • The Google tag or Tag Manager is not tagging site visitors into a remarketing list
  • Active campaigns show no audience segments in their audience tables, in either targeting or observation
You may want to confirm both that the lists exist and that live campaigns attach them. A list sitting unused in the audience manager changes nothing until a campaign references it.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the higher-value reach lists tend to be cart and checkout abandoners and past purchasers since those audiences already showed intent or bought. You may want to confirm the site tags these events into remarketing lists and that a current customer file is uploaded as a Customer Match segment. A store running only on cold reach leaves its repeat-purchase and recovery audiences untouched.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, the reach lists worth confirming are visitors who reached the form but did not submit, and a Customer Match upload of existing contacts or closed customers for either re-engagement or exclusion. You may want to confirm these segments exist and are attached, so the account can re-engage warm prospects and feed automated bidding a signal drawn from people who resemble known leads.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether remarketing and Customer Match lists exist in the audience manager and whether active campaigns attach them in either targeting or observation.

google_ads_audience_manager.label: Google Ads audience manager
steps: Open 'Tools', then 'Shared library', then 'Audience manager', and review 'Your data segments' for remarketing lists and 'Customer lists' for Customer Match segments.
For each segment, note its size and whether it meets the minimum to serve on the networks the account uses. A segment that is too small will show a status that says so.
If the audience manager shows no segments, or only segments too small to serve, the account has no usable first-party reach lists to attach.

google_ads_campaign_audiences.label: Google Ads campaign audiences
steps: Open an active campaign or ad group and open its 'Audiences' table.
Note whether any remarketing or Customer Match segment is attached, and whether it is set to 'Targeting' or 'Observation'.
If no campaign attaches a first-party segment in either mode, the lists, if they exist, are sitting unused.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether remarketing and Customer Match segments exist in the audience manager and are large enough to serve, and whether any active campaign attaches them in targeting or observation. An account with usable first-party segments attached to at least one live campaign passes. An account with no segments built, or with segments built but attached to no active campaign, is a fail recorded with which of the two gaps applies.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether first-party segments exist and whether any active campaign attaches them to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} is running paid campaigns without first-party reach lists in use. Remarketing and Customer Match segments are built from past visitors and existing customers, who tend to convert at a higher rate than cold reach, and attaching them in observation also feeds automated bidding a signal drawn from known converters. {{voice_recommend}} building remarketing lists from site and app activity, uploading a current customer file as a Customer Match segment, and attaching these segments to active campaigns in the mode each campaign needs. After the change, confirm the segments are large enough to serve and that live campaigns reference them.

audit_checks.title: Audience segments are set to Targeting and over-narrowing campaign reach
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An audience attached in 'Targeting' restricts a campaign to that segment, so a segment chosen as a read-only signal but left in targeting can cut a campaign off from most of the traffic it could reach. The campaign keeps spending inside the narrowed pool, and the lost reach looks like low volume rather than a setting that needs changing.

educate.base:
Google Ads attaches an audience segment to a campaign or ad group in one of two modes. 'Targeting' restricts the campaign to serve only to people in that segment. 'Observation' leaves reach unchanged and reports how the segment performs so the practitioner can read it and adjust bids. The two settings look similar in the audience table, and the difference between them decides whether a segment narrows a campaign or only measures it.

The risk shows up when a segment meant as an observation signal is left in targeting. A Search campaign set to target a single remarketing list, for example, then serves only to that list and stops reaching new searchers entirely. The reach collapses to the size of the segment, and the campaign keeps spending inside that smaller pool. Because the campaign still serves and still records conversions, the lost volume reads as a quiet shortfall rather than a restriction someone applied.

The default that fits most prospecting and Search campaigns is observation, which reads the segment without limiting who the campaign can reach. Targeting fits cases where narrowing is the intent, such as a remarketing-only campaign that should serve only to past visitors. The mismatch to check for is a broad campaign carrying a segment in targeting when observation was the intent.

You may want to read each attached segment's mode against the role the campaign is meant to play. A prospecting campaign restricted to a single audience list, or a Search campaign that should reach new queries but is locked to a remarketing segment, are the cases worth a second look.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, a common mismatch is a Search or prospecting campaign locked to a past-purchaser or cart-abandoner segment in targeting, which cuts it off from new shoppers. You may want to confirm that segments meant to grow reach sit in observation, and reserve targeting for campaigns whose purpose is to serve only to existing audiences, such as a dedicated remarketing push.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, a prospecting campaign set to target an existing-contacts segment serves only to people already in the funnel, which works against the goal of finding new prospects. You may want to confirm new-business campaigns attach their audiences in observation, and use targeting only where reaching a known list is the deliberate intent.

investigate.intro: Read the mode on every attached audience segment and check whether any broad campaign is set to 'Targeting' where 'Observation' was the intent.

google_ads_campaign_audiences.label: Google Ads campaign audiences
steps: Open each campaign and ad group and open its 'Audiences' table.
For every attached segment, note whether it is set to 'Targeting' or 'Observation', and note the campaign's intended role, for example prospecting, Search, or a dedicated remarketing campaign.
Flag any broad or prospecting campaign that carries a segment in 'Targeting' because that setting restricts the campaign to that segment rather than reading it.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign or ad group, the attached segments and the mode of each, alongside the campaign's intended role. A campaign whose segment modes match its role passes, for example observation on a prospecting campaign or targeting on a deliberate remarketing-only campaign. A broad or prospecting campaign carrying a segment in targeting where observation was intended is a fail recorded with the campaign and segment names.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the mode of each attached segment and the campaign's intended role to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} has audience segments set to 'Targeting' on campaigns where 'Observation' was the intent, which restricts those campaigns to the attached segment instead of reading it. The reach narrows to the size of the segment, so the campaigns serve and spend inside a smaller pool and the lost volume reads as low traffic rather than a restriction. {{voice_recommend}} switching segments on prospecting and broad-reach campaigns to observation so they measure without narrowing, and reserving targeting for campaigns whose purpose is to serve only to a known audience. After the change, confirm reach recovers on the affected campaigns.

audit_checks.title: Optimized targeting is left on and widening reach past chosen segments
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Optimized targeting is on by default for eligible campaigns and looks beyond the segments a practitioner chose, so a campaign meant to serve a defined audience can spend a share of its budget on users the practitioner never selected. The effect can help or hurt depending on the goal, and left unread it can drift spend away from the intended audience.

educate.base:
Optimized targeting lets a campaign serve beyond the audience segments a practitioner selected, treating those segments as a starting signal and then finding additional users it predicts will convert. It is on by default for Demand Gen, Display, and Video action campaigns, so a campaign can be reaching users outside its chosen segments without anyone turning the feature on.

This widening can help a campaign find converters the chosen segments missed, and it can also pull spend toward users the practitioner deliberately excluded from the segment plan. When a campaign is meant to serve a defined first-party audience, optimized targeting expanding past that audience works against the intent. The setting deserves a deliberate decision rather than acceptance by default. Its value depends on whether broadening reach fits the campaign's goal.

Demographic expansion travels with optimized targeting on eligible campaigns, which means the campaign can also serve past the age, gender, household income, or parental status signals the practitioner set. The audience insights panel reports which segments optimized targeting found and how they performed, so the feature's effect is readable rather than hidden.

You may want to check each eligible campaign for whether optimized targeting is on, whether that fits the campaign's goal, and what the audience insights show it is reaching. A tightly defined first-party campaign and a broad prospecting campaign call for different decisions on this setting.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, optimized targeting on a remarketing or Customer Match campaign can serve past the customer audience the campaign was built around, which dilutes a push meant for known shoppers. You may want to confirm the setting matches the goal, leaving it on where finding new converters is welcome and turning it off where the campaign should stay on its first-party audience.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, optimized targeting can widen a campaign past a qualified-prospect segment toward users who fit the conversion model but not the qualification criteria, which can raise volume while lowering lead quality. You may want to read the audience insights and confirm the broader reach is producing the kind of leads the campaign is meant to bring in.

investigate.intro: Check whether optimized targeting is on for each eligible campaign and read the audience insights to see what it is reaching beyond the chosen segments.

google_ads_campaign_settings.label: Google Ads campaign settings
steps: Open each Demand Gen, Display, or Video action campaign and open the audience settings within the campaign or ad group.
Note whether optimized targeting is on, and whether that fits the campaign's role, for example a defined first-party campaign versus a broad prospecting campaign.
Open the audience insights for the campaign and read which segments optimized targeting surfaced and how they performed against the chosen segments.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per eligible campaign, whether optimized targeting is on and whether that setting matches the campaign's goal. A campaign where the setting is a deliberate fit, on for prospecting that welcomes new reach or off for a defined first-party audience, passes. A campaign where optimized targeting is widening reach past the intended audience without that being the goal is a fail recorded with the campaign name.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether optimized targeting is on for each eligible campaign and whether it fits the campaign's goal to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that optimized targeting is left on for eligible campaigns on {{site_name}} and is widening reach past the audience segments selected for them. The feature is on by default for Demand Gen, Display, and Video action campaigns, so a campaign meant for a defined first-party audience can spend a share of its budget on users outside that audience, and demographic expansion can serve past the demographic signals that were set. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing each eligible campaign, reading its audience insights to see what optimized targeting is reaching, and turning the setting off where the campaign should stay on its chosen audience while leaving it on where broader reach fits the goal. After the change, confirm spend is reaching the intended audience.
